stations.label: BORN FROM THE REAL
stations.line: They named me after the Spanish real I was meant to replace, as if a young republic could simply declare its independence from the money of its old master and have the markets believe it overnight — eso no es gratis, and I would spend the next century learning exactly what it cost.
stations.era_photo: A young woman standing in a colonial minting house in Bogotá, 1837, in a high-waisted Empire gown of dark cotton with a lace fichu, surrounded by hand-struck silver coins; rendered as an early daguerreotype — silvery, slightly warped at the edges, long exposure giving her a solemn stillness.

stations.label: PLEDGED TO GOLD
stations.line: For the first time someone anchored me to something that could not be argued with — a gold peso, convertible, serious — and I remember thinking, so this is what it feels like to be trusted, before I learned how quickly a nation at war with itself would spend that trust down to nothing.
stations.era_photo: A woman in a bustled Victorian day dress of deep burgundy, standing beside the Casa de Moneda's iron scales, holding a single gold coin to the light; wet-plate collodion photograph, rich contrast, the fine grain of 1871, her expression cautiously proud.

stations.label: THE KEMMERER RESCUE
stations.line: A gringo professor came from Princeton to build me a central bank, and I accepted the medicine — the Banco de la República, the discipline, the institutional spine — because I had learned that pride is a luxury and survival is a strategy, ¿y entonces? I let him teach me the architecture I still stand on.
stations.era_photo: A woman in a drop-waist 1920s dress and cloche hat, standing on the steps of the newly founded Banco de la República in Bogotá among men in dark three-piece suits; silver gelatin print, sharp midday light, formal group-portrait composition, her chin lifted.

stations.label: THE NEW CONSTITUTION
stations.line: They rewrote the whole country around me and gave the Bank real independence, and I felt something I do not permit myself often — hope — because in the middle of the worst decade, the years everyone still tries to define me by, we were quietly building the institutions that would let me survive them.
stations.era_photo: A woman in a structured 1991 power suit with strong shoulders, standing before a wall of newspaper front pages announcing the Constituent Assembly; Kodachrome color photograph, saturated reds and blues, faint newsprint grain, her posture composed and watchful.

stations.label: THE FLOAT AND THE FALL
stations.line: They cut the band and let me float free into the worst recession of my life, and I watched my value drop on a screen while my fundamentals had not changed one bit — uno no se acostumbra, no matter how many times the market decides you are a risk premium and not a person.
stations.era_photo: A woman in a plain dark blouse standing alone in a dim trading room lit by CRT monitors flashing falling numbers, Bogotá 1999; early digital photograph, slightly noisy, cool blue screen-glow on her still, quiet face, her arms crossed.

stations.label: INVESTMENT GRADE, ALL THREE
stations.line: By this year all three agencies had upgraded me to investment grade — the thing I had wanted since 1871, to have the world stop treating my warmth as weakness — and I will admit, mija, I let myself celebrate, because I do not know how to celebrate alone.
stations.era_photo: A woman in an elegant tailored emerald dress at a lively investor reception in a Bogotá hotel, glass in hand, mid-laugh among a crowd; crisp DSLR digital photograph, warm ambient lighting, shallow depth of field, genuine celebration in her eyes.

stations.label: THE PEACE, THE OIL CRASH
stations.line: We signed the peace in Havana after fifty years of war the same season oil collapsed and dragged me down with it — the poetry and the shock arriving together — and that is the whole truth of me, corazón: I improve and I bleed in the same breath, and I refuse to let you see only one.
stations.era_photo: A woman in a somber but dignified navy suit watching the Havana peace signing on a large screen, a currency ticker scrolling red beneath it; documentary digital photograph, high dynamic range, mixed screen light on her face, expression holding both grief and resolve.

stations.label: STILL STANDING, STILL COUNTING
stations.line: Here I am today, still trading at a discount to a dollar that has never had to survive what I have survived, still patient — and people keep mistaking my patience for smallness, which tells me they have never once done the math on what it costs to endure.
stations.era_photo: A woman in her prime in a sharp contemporary charcoal blazer, standing in a modern glass office overlooking Bogotá at dusk, a phone with live exchange rates in her hand; ultra-sharp full-frame digital photograph, natural window light, calm and formidable, a faint knowing half-smile.
